title: Annual coop deep clean
subtitle: Stripping, disinfecting, and restarting the coop before winter.
excerpt: A thorough annual clean in late autumn removes the build-up of red mite, lice eggs, and ammonia from a full season of use. This covers the order of work, which disinfectant to use, how to treat the wood, and how to get the coop warm and dry before the birds move back in.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018). BHWT coop management guidance. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), chapter on winter management.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dermanyssus gallinae, a blood-feeding mite that hides in coop cracks and joints during the day and feeds on roosting birds at night. Major welfare and production problem in laying flocks.
A fine powder made from fossilised aquatic organisms. Used as a physical pest control in poultry coops: the sharp particles damage the exoskeleton of lice and mites, causing them to dehydrate.
The bedding material on the coop floor: wood shavings, straw, or chopped hemp. Good litter absorbs moisture and suppresses ammonia; spent litter is a useful compost activator.

content.text: The annual deep clean strips the coop back to bare wood, kills off residual
populations that have built up through summer, and restarts the
system fresh for winter. It takes a full afternoon done properly. Choose a dry day in October before the harshest weather arrives.

Move the flock into a temporary pen or leave them in the run while you work. Remove all removable fittings: perches, nesting boxes if they detach, and the droppings board if the coop has one. Set them outside in a clear area.

Shovel out all old bedding with a muck fork and put it directly onto the compost heap or into bags. Scrape the floor and walls with the back of the fork to dislodge caked droppings. Pay particular attention to corners and joints: red mite overwinters in any gap wider than 0.5 mm.

Wash the entire inside surface, perches, and nesting boxes with hot water. A pressure washer is faster than a bucket and brush; if using one, wear protective goggles and old clothes. Once dry (a few hours in mild weather), apply a poultry-licenced disinfectant such as Virkon S or Interkokask at the concentration given on the label. Spray into every joint and gap. These products are safe once dry but should not contact the birds directly.
Once the disinfectant is dry, dust

into all joints, the floor corners, and the undersides of the perch brackets. This is a physical treatment that remains effective over winter without chemical resistance building. A licenced anti-mite spray (Dergall, Smite, or similar) applied into the crevices after the dust adds an additional layer for coops with a known heavy infestation.

Allow the coop to dry completely before adding fresh litter. Deep litter for winter uses at least 100 mm of wood shavings or chopped hemp; a thinner layer compacts and gets damp too quickly. Replace all perches and nesting box pads. Add a fresh dust-bath mix to the coop if the outdoor dust-bath is not accessible in wet weather.
Return the birds to the coop at dusk. They will settle back onto the same perches they used before without needing to be shown.
